You are implementing **E-1393** — first narrative-kind carve-out from `session_statuses` JSON into a dedicated table. Sets the pattern for subsequent narrative kinds (observations, runbooks, etc. via E-1394 and beyond). Implements E-1352 (events-as-primitive); follows E-1322's commit `4652b1a` for the table+handler pattern.

## What this is

Today, `endless session status add` parses an XML payload with multiple aspects bundled into one row of `session_statuses`. The `decisions` aspect lands in `session_statuses.decisions` as a JSON array. Under E-1352's events-as-primitive direction, decisions become their own first-class kind:

- New event kind: `session.decision_recorded`
- New table: `session_decisions(session_id, decision_text, created_at)`
- New CLI: `endless session decision add "<text>"`

The existing batch wrapup XML continues to work; the handler should fan out the `<decision>` elements into `session_decisions` rows (one per element) in addition to (or instead of) populating `session_statuses.decisions`. **Discuss at plan time**: dual-write (both old JSON column + new table) for backward compat, or new-table-only (eventually drop the column). Pick the simpler path that doesn't break existing reads.

## Schema (V12 migration)

```sql
CREATE TABLE session_decisions (
    id           INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    session_id   INTEGER NOT NULL,
    decision_text TEXT   NOT NULL,
    created_at   TEXT    NOT NULL
);
CREATE INDEX idx_session_decisions_session ON session_decisions(session_id, created_at DESC);
```

This one DOES get a surrogate `id` PK — decisions are append-only events with no natural compound key (the same session can record many decisions; the text isn't a key). Unlike session_tasks where the (session, task) pair was naturally unique.

NO FK on session_id per the materialized-index-outlives-entities rationale.

## Event kind

`internal/events/event.go`:
```go
KindSessionDecisionRecorded Kind = "session.decision_recorded"
```

Payload:
```go
type SessionDecisionRecordedPayload struct {
    Text string `json:"text"`
}
```

## Handler

`internal/events/executor.go`: `execSessionDecisionRecorded`. INSERT one row into session_decisions.

Plus: in the existing `execSessionStatusRecorded` handler, when parsing the wrapup XML's `<decisions>` element, ALSO emit one `session.decision_recorded` event per `<decision>` child (or directly INSERT — the simpler path; emitting from within a handler is layering work that may not be worth it). The cleanest path is probably: in execSessionStatusRecorded, after the parent row insert, loop the decisions and INSERT into session_decisions directly. The events JSONL gets just one `session_status.recorded` event (its existing shape); the materialized fan-out lives in the handler.

## CLI surface

```
endless session decision add "<text>"

Options:
  --session N    Session id (default: current pane)
```

Output: confirmation + decision id.

Plus the symmetric read:

```
endless session decisions [OPTIONS]

Options:
  --session N    Session id (default: current pane)
  --since <when>
  --limit N
  --format [table|json]
```

Output: id / created_at / decision text (truncated for table view).

The `add` verb writes via event emit (consistent with other writes); the read goes through a Go monitor function (E-894).

## Pattern for subsequent narrative kinds

This task SETS THE PATTERN. Subsequent narrative kinds (observations via E-1394; runbooks/conclusions if/when filed) should follow the same shape:

- Surrogate id PK (these are append-only event-rows, no natural compound key)
- INSERT-only handler (no UPSERT; each event is a discrete record)
- `add` write verb + plural read verb (`decisions` listing for the singular `decision`)
- Fan-out hook from `execSessionStatusRecorded` if the wrapup XML carries the kind

Document this pattern in the commit message so E-1394's implementer can mirror it without rediscovery.

For execSessionDecisionRecorded: same guard as session_tasks: `evt.Actor.SessionID != ""`. No `Kind == ActorSession` check. See LESSONS.md "Verify the actor model before specifying actor-based guards."

You are implementing **E-1396** — add a surrogate `id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY` column to the existing `session_tasks` table. E-1322 landed without one; the commit message defended the omission, but the omission violates a non-negotiable house rule. All tables get an id PK. No exceptions.

## What this is

`session_tasks` exists today (from E-1322, commit `4652b1a` on main) with this schema:

```sql
CREATE TABLE session_tasks (
    session_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    task_id    INTEGER NOT NULL,
    created_at TEXT    NOT NULL,
    updated_at TEXT    NOT NULL,
    UNIQUE(session_id, task_id)
);
```

The rule (LESSONS.md entry "Every table gets a surrogate `id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`") requires `id` as the first column. UNIQUE compound stays as a constraint, NOT as the primary key.

## The fix

SQLite can't `ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN` with a `PRIMARY KEY` clause. The migration is the standard SQLite table-rebuild dance:

```sql
CREATE TABLE session_tasks_new (
    id         INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    session_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    task_id    INTEGER NOT NULL,
    created_at TEXT    NOT NULL,
    updated_at TEXT    NOT NULL,
    UNIQUE(session_id, task_id)
);

INSERT INTO session_tasks_new (session_id, task_id, created_at, updated_at)
SELECT session_id, task_id, created_at, updated_at FROM session_tasks
ORDER BY created_at;  -- preserves insertion order in the new surrogate ids

DROP TABLE session_tasks;
ALTER TABLE session_tasks_new RENAME TO session_tasks;

CREATE INDEX idx_session_tasks_task ON session_tasks(task_id);
```

ORDER BY created_at in the INSERT-SELECT matters: SQLite assigns `id` in insertion order, so older touches get smaller ids. Without the ORDER BY the order is engine-defined.

- A house-rule note. The natural home: a new section in `~/Projects/endless/docs/` or a Go skill. Or extend `CLAUDE.md` (the project one in the repo). The rule: "Every CREATE TABLE in this project gets `id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY` as the first column; UNIQUE constraints go AFTER. No exceptions." You are implementing **E-1401 (FIVE-ALARM)** — gate event emission on resolvable session_id. The current Python emit_event path silently drops `session_id` from the actor block when the resolver returns None. Result: events fire with no attribution, downstream materialized tables lose data, the system silently degrades.

Mike: "Inability to determine a valid session ID and to do so accurately and robustness should be treated as a FIVE ALARM FIRE!"

## The bug (concrete)

`src/endless/event_bridge.py:16` `emit_event()` does this:

```python
if session_id is None:
    try:
        from endless.task_cmd import _current_endless_session_id
        eid = _current_endless_session_id()
        if eid is not None:
            session_id = str(eid)
    except Exception:
        session_id = None
```

When `_current_endless_session_id()` returns None (which happens with multiple sibling Claude panes, per E-1294's documented "0 or 2+ falls through to None" behavior), `session_id` stays None, the event emits without it, and the downstream Go handlers see an empty `Actor.SessionID`. The E-1322/E-1381 guard correctly skips, but the cost is silent activity loss.

## The fix

After resolution attempt, if the actor kind warrants session attribution AND `session_id` is still None, **raise `click.ClickException` with an actionable message**. Do NOT emit the event.

Gate logic:

```python
ATTRIBUTION_REQUIRED = {"cli", "hook"}

if actor_kind in ATTRIBUTION_REQUIRED and not session_id:
    raise click.ClickException(
        "Cannot determine the Endless session for this pane. "
        "Run `endless task bind <task-id>` to bind this pane to a task, "
        "or pass session_id explicitly. "
        "Silent attribution loss is not acceptable; refusing to emit this event. "
        "(E-1401)"
    )
```

ActorKind not in the set (e.g., `system`, `web`):
- `system`: cron-style invocations may legitimately have no Claude session attribution; allow empty session_id.
- `web`: web UI users have user_id attribution, not session attribution; allow empty session_id.

## Tests

In `tests/test_event_bridge.py` (or wherever event_bridge has tests; create the file if needed):

1. Mock `_current_endless_session_id` to return None, call emit_event with `actor_kind="cli"`. Assert `click.ClickException` raised.
2. Same with `actor_kind="hook"`. Assert raised.
3. Same with `actor_kind="system"`. Assert event emits (no exception, no session_id in actor).
4. Same with `actor_kind="web"`. Assert event emits.
5. With resolver returning a real id, `actor_kind="cli"`. Assert event emits with session_id populated.
6. With explicit session_id argument (bypassing resolver), `actor_kind="cli"`, resolver returns None. Assert event emits with the explicit session_id (the gate is only on resolver-derived None).

You are implementing **E-1402 (FIVE-ALARM pair)** — strengthen the session resolver so it picks a session deterministically in the multi-sibling pane case, instead of giving up and returning None. Pairs with E-1401 (refuse-to-emit gate); both are required for the FIVE-ALARM fix to be complete.

## The bug (concrete)

`src/endless/task_cmd.py:1971` `_current_endless_session_id()` calls `_find_sibling_claude_session()` (line 2012) which returns:

- `(None, 0)` — no sibling Claude session
- `(eid, 1)` — exactly one match → use it
- `(None, n)` — n>1 matches → ambiguous, return None

Today's failure: with 5+ active Claude sessions in sibling panes, the n>1 branch fires, the resolver returns None, and the downstream emit_event silently emits attribution-less events (until E-1401 lands). Mike's session ran for an entire conversation losing attribution because of this branch.

## The fix

When n>1 sibling matches exist, pick the most-recently-active session instead of giving up. Concretely:

```python
def _find_sibling_claude_session() -> tuple[int | None, int]:
    # ... existing setup unchanged ...
    if not matches:
        return None, 0
    if len(matches) == 1:
        return matches[0]["endless_session_id"], 1
    # E-1402: multi-sibling case. Pick most-recently-active.
    # Query sessions.last_activity for the candidate eids; sort DESC; pick first.
    candidate_eids = [m["endless_session_id"] for m in matches]
    from endless import db
    placeholders = ",".join("?" * len(candidate_eids))
    rows = db.query(
        f"SELECT id FROM sessions "
        f"WHERE id IN ({placeholders}) AND state != 'ended' "
        f"ORDER BY last_activity DESC, id DESC LIMIT 1",
        tuple(candidate_eids),
    )
    if rows:
        return rows[0]["id"], 1
    # Defense in depth: if the DB lookup returns nothing (e.g., all ended),
    # behave as the old contract did.
    return None, len(matches)
```

**Tie-breaker: `id DESC`.** When two sessions have the same `last_activity` (rare but possible at second precision), pick the higher id (more recently created). Deterministic, no flakiness.

**Filter `state != 'ended'`.** A sibling with state=ended shouldn't be picked — those are dead sessions that happen to have a companion file lingering.

**Document the precedence rule** in the docstring of both `_find_sibling_claude_session` and `_current_endless_session_id`. The old docstring's "0 or 2+ falls through to None" is now wrong for the 2+ case.

## Caller compatibility

`_find_sibling_claude_session` has two callers per the docstring:
- `_current_endless_session_id` (the resolver entry point) — wants n=1 semantics, now gets them deterministically more often.
- `claim_item` / `bind_item` (per the docstring) — these want to *refuse* on ambiguity, with a policy refusal pointing at E-1244.

**You must NOT break the policy-refusal callers.** Two options:

A. Add an optional parameter `strict_unique=False`. When `True`, preserves the old behavior (return `(None, n)` on ambiguity). `claim_item` / `bind_item` pass `strict_unique=True`. Resolver entry points use the default `False` and get the new deterministic pick.

B. Split into two functions: `_find_sibling_claude_session_strict` (old behavior, used by claim/bind) and `_find_sibling_claude_session_pick` (new behavior, used by resolver).

Pick whichever fits the codebase better. Option A is one signature, two callers. Option B is two functions, clearer at call sites.

## Tests

In `tests/test_task_cmd.py` (or wherever session-resolver tests live):

1. 0 matches → `(None, 0)`. (Existing behavior preserved.)
2. 1 match → `(eid, 1)`. (Existing behavior preserved.)
3. 2+ matches with distinct last_activity → returns the most recent eid as `(eid, 1)`. (New behavior.)
4. 2+ matches with identical last_activity → returns the higher-id eid. (Tie-breaker.)
5. 2+ matches but all with `state='ended'` → returns `(None, n)`. (Filter applied.)
6. Strict mode (whichever knob you implement) → preserves old `(None, n)` semantics. (Policy-refusal callers.)

## E2E verification

The real-world scenario: from a Claude pane with multiple sibling Claude panes alive.

```
tail -1 .endless/db-ledger/$(ls -t .endless/db-ledger/ | head -1)

```

Without E-1401 in place, this is the only way to verify. If E-1401 has landed and gates emission, the test setup needs to ensure the resolver succeeds (otherwise the gate fires before you can observe success).

## Pairing with E-1401

E-1401 errors when the resolver returns None. E-1402 reduces how often that happens by making n>1 cases resolve. Both ship for the FIVE-ALARM fix to be complete:
- E-1402 alone: silent degradation in the truly impossible cases (0 sibling, all ended).
- E-1401 alone: gate fires correctly but is annoying because the multi-sibling case is common.
- Both: gate fires only when no defensible choice exists.

## House rules

# Pre-rebase check in `worktree land`: abort with a named-files error when `.endless/` is dirty

## Problem

`endless worktree land` invokes `git rebase` without first checking for
uncommitted or untracked files under `.endless/`. When the working tree
is dirty, rebase fails partway with a generic "You have unstaged changes"
error — no file list, no recovery hint.

## Why this is a safety net, not the primary fix

The original failure (snapshot files left untracked by `task update
--status verify`) is being resolved structurally by E-1361 / E-1362 /
E-1363 / E-1364, which move plan snapshots and verbs.json out of the
working tree into `<main>/.git/info/endless/`. After those land, the
snapshot writer no longer dirties `.endless/`.

This task adds a check in `worktree land` that catches any OTHER
`.endless/` writer that might leave the tree dirty — a future writer
added but missed during the E-1361 rollout, a manual edit, a
partially-staged plan file. Instead of git's generic error, the user
sees the file list and the fix.

## Scope

In `endless worktree land`, before invoking `git rebase`:

1. Run `git status --porcelain` on the worktree.
2. If any uncommitted (modified, staged, or untracked) entries exist
   under `.endless/`:
   - Abort the land with an error that lists the files AND names the fix
     command (e.g.
     `git add <files> && git commit -m "Endless: record ledger entry"`).
   - Do NOT auto-commit.
3. Uncommitted entries outside `.endless/` retain today's behavior
   (rebase surfaces its own message).

## Out of scope

- Commit-at-write-site for snapshots and verbs.json — resolved by E-1361.
- Auto-committing dirty files inside `worktree land` — rejected;
  attribution belongs with the writing operation, not with land.
- Detecting dirty trees outside `worktree land`.

## Verification

1. From a worktree, create an untracked file under `.endless/`
   (e.g. `touch .endless/scratch.txt`).
2. Run `endless worktree land <id>`.
3. Land must abort BEFORE rebase, with an error message that:
   - Names `.endless/scratch.txt` explicitly.
   - Shows the commit command to fix.
4. Commit the file manually, re-run land — must succeed.
5. Repeat with a modified-but-unstaged `.endless/` file — same behavior.
6. Untracked file outside `.endless/` — land does NOT abort early;
   rebase's native handling proceeds.

You are picking up E-1417. Goal: make `endless worktree land` surface real content-conflict failures clearly instead of burying them inside an "orphan auto-amend cleanup failed" message that misnames the failing step.

## The bug, concretely

`_drop_orphan_amendable_commits` at `src/endless/worktree_cmd.py:255` runs:

```
git rebase --onto base_branch last_orphan_sha
```

That single rebase command does two things in sequence:
  1. Drops the contiguous orphan auto-amend commits at branch base (the named purpose).
  2. Replays the user's actual work commits onto `base_branch`.

When step 2 hits a content conflict (a real user-code conflict, because `main` advanced with overlapping edits while the branch was in progress), the call site at `src/endless/worktree_cmd.py:957-966` catches `CalledProcessError`, runs `git rebase --abort`, and raises:

```
orphan auto-amend cleanup failed: <stderr blob>
```

Two distinct usability bugs:
  - **Wrong step named.** Orphan-cleanup actually succeeded — the orphan was identified, the rebase started, the orphan got dropped. What failed is the *replay* of the user's commits. The user is sent looking in the wrong place.
  - **Filenames buried.** The conflicting paths are inside `stderr` (often hundreds of characters of git hint text). Easy to miss; harder to act on.

Plus a third concern: after the failure, the worktree is left in a clean `git status` (rebase aborted), so the user has nothing to continue from. They have to either run `git rebase main` manually in the worktree and re-run `land`, or know to ask. The current code tells them neither.

Recent real-world repro: E-1402's land hit this when E-1401 landed on `main` mid-stream and both branches touched `src/endless/event_bridge.py`. Mike was the user; the prompt you're reading is the follow-up he filed.

## Sibling task

**E-1416** covers a related-but-distinct failure mode: *uncommitted* ledger artifacts trigger a generic "unstaged changes" error from the same `land` codepath. Same usability umbrella, different root cause. Read E-1416 before you start so the two fixes share a coherent error-surface story. A session is already in flight on E-1416 — check `endless task show E-1416 --children` before assuming anything about ownership.

## Phase 1 — investigation (before designing)

- Read `_drop_orphan_amendable_commits` (`src/endless/worktree_cmd.py:255`) end-to-end.
- Read `land_worktree`'s Step 3.7 call site (`src/endless/worktree_cmd.py:952-972`) and Step 4's rebase block (`src/endless/worktree_cmd.py:974-995`). Note that Step 4 already has a different, more informative error message — borrowing its phrasing may be the right move for Step 3.7.
- Find existing tests around `worktree land` rebase failures: `tests/test_worktree_land_*.py`. Identify which failure modes are covered and which aren't.
- Inspect: what does `git status --porcelain` and the `.git/rebase-merge/` directory look like at the moment of failure? That state determines whether option 2 below (leave-in-conflict) is viable.
- Determine: when the orphan-drop *and* the user-commit replay both fail in the same rebase, can git tell us which one was the conflicting commit? `git status` during the conflict names the commit that failed to apply — capture that.

## Phase 2 — design

Produce a plan file at `.endless/plans/E-1417.md` answering, at minimum:

1. **Detect-and-rephrase, leave-in-conflict, or split-rebase?** Three families of fix:

   a. **Detect-and-rephrase only.** Catch the `CalledProcessError`, look at the rebase output to distinguish orphan-conflict from user-code-conflict, emit a clearer error with the filenames and a recovery path. Run `git rebase --abort` as today. *Pros:* smallest blast radius. *Cons:* user still has to run a manual rebase outside `land` and then re-run, which feels clunky.

   b. **Leave-in-conflict.** When the failure is a real content conflict in user code, *don't* abort. Leave the worktree in the rebase-in-progress state, emit instructions: resolve, `git add`, `git rebase --continue`, then re-run `endless worktree land`. Land would need to detect "rebase already in progress" at the top and pick up where it left off. *Pros:* fewer manual steps for the user. *Cons:* land becomes stateful; what does verbs.json dedup (Step 3.5) look like on a re-entry? What if the user `git rebase --abort`s on their own?

   c. **Split into two rebases.** Separate the orphan-drop from the user-commit replay so the error paths are distinguishable structurally instead of by parsing stderr. *Pros:* clean error attribution, no parsing. *Cons:* extra rebase invocation (perf?), and the two-rebase sequence may itself need rollback handling.

   Pick the family Mike approves and elaborate.

2. **What goes in the error message?** Filenames, suggested git commands, both? Per house rules: command lines that the user will copy must be on a single physical line in the rendered output. No emojis. No internal task IDs.

3. **Test coverage.** What scenarios? Suggested baseline:
   - Orphan-only conflict (today's intended target of the helper) → succeeds, no error.
   - User-code conflict without orphan → currently hits Step 4's path; verify it still does and the message is good.
   - Orphan + user-code conflict in the same rebase → the bug being fixed; new error message asserted.
   - (If 2b chosen) Re-run of `land` while a rebase is already in progress → completes the rebase and proceeds.

4. **Interaction with E-1416.** Should the two fixes share a helper / error-message style / recovery-path template? Or stay independent? Coordinate with the in-flight E-1416 session before splitting style.

Each option gets pros/cons. **DO NOT CHOOSE — present to Mike and have him decide.**

## Phase 3 — implementation and closure

Once Mike approves the plan:

- Implement on `task/1417-...`.
- Add the tests from Phase 2's coverage list.
- Drive to land + verify, not just "PR ready." If land itself fails on the way out (the very bug you're fixing), you have a real-world repro — use it.
- Verify handoff message must include the specific test setup: "To verify, create a branch that touches a file recently modified on main, then run `endless worktree land`; expect <new error wording> with filenames on their own line."

## Non-goals

- Fixing E-1416's uncommitted-artifact failure mode (separate task, separate session).
- Auto-resolving conflicts.
- Rewriting `_drop_orphan_amendable_commits`'s orphan-detection logic — the bug is in error surfacing, not detection.
- Adding interactive prompts to `land` (e.g., asking the user "want me to abort or stay?"). Land is non-interactive by contract; the error message is the interface.

## Closing

- Status flow: `verify` when implementation is done and tested, with the verify message above. Do NOT mark `confirmed`.
- Do NOT auto-land. Ask Mike when ready.
Run 'endless guide' and then wait

# Rework `endless task claim` output: fix broken `eswt` reference, hide sandbox path, align labels

## Problems

### 1. The output instructs users to run a command that does not exist

Current output:

```
Updated E-1424 (Test foo):
• Status: needs_plan -> in_progress
• bound to session 1

• sandbox provisioned: ~/.cache/endless/sandboxes/worktree-e-1424
• worktree created: ~/Projects/endless/.endless/worktrees/e-1424

  To work on this task, choose one:
    1. Delegate to a fresh Claude session:
         endless task spawn E-1424
    2. Do it yourself (edit, run tests, etc.):
         eval "$(endless shell-init)"  # Adds eswt shell helper func
         eswt E-1424                   # Changes to Git worktree dir
```

`endless shell-init` defines exactly three helpers: `esu`, `esp`, `esf`. There is no `eswt`. The comment "Adds eswt shell helper func" is false, and `eswt E-1424` produces "command not found." The correct helper is `esu` (= `endless session use`), which cd's into the worktree AND exports `ENDLESS_SESSION_ID` — exactly the intended action.

### 2. The sandbox cache path is presented as if it were a cd target

The output lists `sandbox provisioned: ~/.cache/endless/sandboxes/worktree-e-1424` as a parallel line to `worktree created: ...`. Users scan for a path and cd into the first one — the sandbox cache dir — which is never where they should be. Subsequent endless commands then fail with `Error: Not in a registered project directory` because the cache dir is not a project. Hit by Mike 2026-05-19.

The sandbox path has no routine user-facing purpose (only a rare SQL-client-debug case). It should not appear in default output.

## Deliverables

### A. Fix the broken helper reference
Replace `eswt` with `esu` and correct the comment. `esu E-1424` is the cd-to-worktree + export-session action.

### B. Hide the sandbox path from default output
Remove the sandbox cache path line. Add a dedicated command to retrieve it on demand:

> `endless worktree sandbox [E-NNN]` — prints the absolute sandbox cache directory path for a worktree's task, for the rare SQL-client-debug case. Matches the existing pattern of small print verbs (`endless session id`, `endless session cd`). With no arg, resolves from cwd if inside a worktree.

### C. Adopt the aligned-label format

Proposed by Mike 2026-05-19:

```
Updated E-1424 (Test foo):

  • Status:       needs_plan -> in_progress
  • Session:      1
  • Git worktree: ~/Projects/endless/.endless/worktrees/e-1424

  To work on this task, choose one:

    1. Delegate to a fresh Claude session:
         endless task spawn E-1424

    2. Work in sibling tmux pane (edit, run tests, etc.):
         eval "$(endless shell-init)"  # Adds esu/esp/esf shell helpers
         esu E-1424                    # cd to worktree + activate session

```

Changes from current:
- Aligned labels (`Status`, `Session`, `Git worktree`) — scannable.
- Sandbox path removed entirely (see deliverable B).
- "Session: 1" in the labeled column instead of "bound to session 1".
- "Work in sibling tmux pane" replaces vague "Do it yourself".
- `esu E-1424` replaces the non-existent `eswt E-1424`; comment corrected.
- Blank lines between numbered options; trailing newline added.

### D. Apply the same labeling discipline to sibling commands
`endless task spawn` and `endless task release` have analogous multi-line output. Audit and bring them to the same aligned-label format. Verify their work-instruction blocks reference real helpers (no `eswt`-style ghosts).

## Verification

1. `endless task claim E-NNNN` from main checkout → output uses the aligned format, no sandbox path, references `esu`.
2. Copy the `esu E-NNNN` line verbatim into a shell that has run `eval "$(endless shell-init)"` → it cd's into the worktree and exports `ENDLESS_SESSION_ID`.
3. `endless worktree sandbox E-NNNN` → prints the sandbox cache path, exit 0.
4. `endless task spawn` / `endless task release` outputs → same aligned format, no broken helper references.

## Open question for Mike

The proposed format still shows `Session: 1`. Confirm whether the session id belongs in claim output at all, or whether it's noise now that `esu` handles session activation. Present the trade-off; do not drop it unilaterally.

# Validate ENDLESS_SESSION_ID against the sessions table before trusting it

## Problem

The env-var layer of `_current_endless_session_id` (`src/endless/task_cmd.py`) accepts any digit string in `ENDLESS_SESSION_ID` and returns it as the current session id without checking that a matching row exists in the `sessions` table:

```python
env_id = os.environ.get("ENDLESS_SESSION_ID")
if env_id and env_id.isdigit():
    return int(env_id)
```

A bogus or stale value (`ENDLESS_SESSION_ID=999999`, or a real id from a since-ended session) passes the E-1401 attribution gate in `event_bridge.py` — the gate only checks that `session_id` is non-empty, not that it's valid — and every event then gets attributed to a non-existent or wrong session. Silent misattribution.

## Origin

This was originally part of E-1415 ("Validate resolved session id against DB in `_current_endless_session_id`"). E-1415 was set to `obsolete`: its companion-file-validation portion is subsumed by E-1426 (which retires companion files — once the resolver reads `sessions.process` directly, a row read from the DB inherently exists). The **env-var layer is independent of companion files** and that concern survives E-1415's obsoletion — hence this dedicated task.

## Fix

In `_current_endless_session_id`, after confirming `ENDLESS_SESSION_ID` is a digit string, look up the id in the `sessions` table. If no row exists:
- Treat the env-var layer as not-resolved (fall through to the next resolution layer).
- Write a one-line warning to stderr naming the bad value: e.g. `warning: ENDLESS_SESSION_ID=999999 does not match any session; ignoring.`

Do not silently accept it, and do not silently discard it without telling the user — a stale env var in their shell is something they need to know about and fix.

## Coordination

- **E-1426** (urgent, in progress) rewrites `_current_endless_session_id`'s companion/process layer. This task touches the same function's *env-var* layer (layer 1, which runs before the process lookup). Whoever holds E-1426 should be aware this validation is landing in the same function; sequence to avoid a merge collision.
- **E-1401** is the attribution gate this validation protects. The gate itself stays a presence check; this task makes the resolver stop feeding it invalid ids.

## Verification

1. `ENDLESS_SESSION_ID=999999 endless task add "x"` from a context with no other resolution layer available → resolver ignores the bogus value, stderr warns, and the E-1401 gate refuses (no misattribution).
2. `ENDLESS_SESSION_ID=<valid live id>` → resolver returns it, no warning.
3. `ENDLESS_SESSION_ID=<id of an ended session>` → treated as invalid (decide during planning whether "ended" counts as invalid, or only "no row at all"; present the choice to Mike).
4. Tests covering valid / non-existent / non-digit env-var values.

- Adding a new top-level guide section.
- Documentation in CLAUDE.md or README.md.

## Verification

1. `endless guide tasks` shows the new content alongside existing material.
2. `endless guide --list` does NOT show a new top-level section.
3. Concepts (curated, --inferred, lanes, priority, pending_triage) introduced once and referenced consistently.
4. No regression: existing tasks-section content still present and accurate.

## Instructions

Read E-1421's plan in full and the existing `tasks` guide section. Draft additions that integrate with existing flow (likely a new "Curated 'next' list" subsection within `tasks`). No separate plan file. Flip to `verify` with the verification block.

# E-1453 prompt: Fix `migrate-db -dry-run` silently applying because `monitor.DB()` auto-migrates on open

## Symptom

`endless-event migrate-db -dry-run` reports `{"applied":null,"skipped":[...,"already applied"]}` instead of `{"applied":[...,"dry-run"],"skipped":[]}` — but the database has actually been migrated. Discovered during E-1396 verification: a `-dry-run` invocation against a DB at `user_version=10` returned all-skipped, yet `user_version` came out at `11` and `session_tasks` had the new id column.

Reproducer:

```sh
# Against a DB at user_version=N where N < CurrentSchemaVersion:
endless-event migrate-db -dry-run
# Output suggests no-op. Real effect: full migration applied.
endless sql "SELECT * FROM pragma_user_version()"
# user_version has advanced to CurrentSchemaVersion.
```

## Root cause

`internal/monitor/db.go` — `DB()` opens the connection and unconditionally invokes `migrate(dbConn, MigrateOpts{Runner: RunnerAuto})`:

```go
func DB() (*sql.DB, error) {
    dbOnce.Do(func() {
        ...
        if _, err := migrate(dbConn, MigrateOpts{Runner: RunnerAuto}); err != nil {
            dbErr = fmt.Errorf("auto-migrate %s: %w", path, err)
            ...
        }
    })
    return dbConn, dbErr
}
```

`cmd/endless-event/main.go` reads the `-dry-run` flag, but by the time it calls `monitor.DB()` to get a handle, the real migration has already run via the embedded auto-migrate. The framework's `DryRun` field works correctly for the explicit `Migrate(opts)` path; it's the auto-migrate-on-first-open path that ignores it. So every CLI verb that needs the connection (which is all of them) silently advances the schema before any flag is consulted.

## Fix: plumb a dry-run signal into `DB()`

Change `DB()` to accept `MigrateOpts` (or the subset that affects auto-migrate behavior). The CLI parses flags first, then calls `DB(opts)`. Auto-migrate is skipped or runs dry per the caller's intent.

Sketch:

```go
// Existing call sites continue to work via a thin wrapper:
func DB() (*sql.DB, error) { return DBWith(MigrateOpts{Runner: RunnerAuto}) }

// New entry point that respects the caller's intent:
func DBWith(opts MigrateOpts) (*sql.DB, error) {
    dbOnce.Do(func() {
        ...
        if _, err := migrate(dbConn, opts); err != nil { ... }
    })
    return dbConn, dbErr
}
```

`cmd/endless-event/main.go` becomes:

```go
flag.Parse() // includes -dry-run
opts := MigrateOpts{Runner: RunnerAuto, DryRun: *dryRun}
db, err := monitor.DBWith(opts)
```

Other CLIs that don't care continue to call `monitor.DB()` and get today's behavior.

### Implementation notes

- `dbOnce.Do` is a `sync.Once`, so the first caller's opts win. If two callers race with different opts, the second silently gets the first's behavior. In practice each binary process has one entry point setting opts, so this is fine — but the docstring should call it out.
- The other `MigrateOpts` fields (`AllowRebuild`, `SkipBackup`, `Target`) can ride along on the same plumbing for free; `migrate-db`'s `-force-rebuild` and `-target` flags become non-noops via the same mechanism.

## Verification

1. Register a synthetic migration that mutates the schema noticeably (e.g., `CREATE TABLE _dryrun_canary`).
2. Take a DB at `user_version = CurrentSchemaVersion - 1`.
3. Run `endless-event migrate-db -dry-run`.
4. Assert: `pragma user_version` unchanged, `_dryrun_canary` does NOT exist, and the dry-run report lists the migration with reason `"dry-run"`.
5. Run `endless-event migrate-db` (no flag) and confirm the migration applies normally.
6. Add a regression test in `internal/monitor/migrate_test.go` that exercises the dry-run path against a non-final-version DB.

## Related

- E-1396 — surfaced this bug during V11 verification.
- E-1380 (referenced in migrate.go comments) — migration error-handling hygiene, separate scope.

# E-1454 prompt: Retire `RequiresRebuild` framework gate

## Symptom

When a registered migration sets `RequiresRebuild: true`, **every** fresh DB (tests, new installs) fails to open with `migration requires --force-rebuild`. There is no way for a fresh DB to skip a rebuild that would be a no-op against its just-created tables. Discovered during E-1396 when V11 was first written with `RequiresRebuild: true` (the original plan called for it as belt-and-suspenders alongside `BEGIN IMMEDIATE TRANSACTION`); the change immediately broke 53 fresh-DB tests, forcing the flag to be dropped and the migration to rely on transaction atomicity alone.

Reproducer (synthetic):

```go
// Add to migrations registry:
{Version: 12, Name: "foo", Apply: migrateV12, RequiresRebuild: true},
```

Run `just test`. Every Python test that opens a fresh DB fails with `auto-migrate ...: migration requires --force-rebuild`.

## Root cause

`internal/monitor/migrate.go:263-270`:

```go
if m.RequiresRebuild && !opts.AllowRebuild {
    err = doterr.NewErr(
        ErrRequiresRebuild,
        doterr.IntKV("version", m.Version),
        doterr.StringKV("name", m.Name),
    )
    goto end
}
```

The gate is a flat boolean check. It does not consult:

- Whether the current DB actually has the OLD-shape table that needs rebuilding.
- Whether this is a fresh DB (`user_version = 0`) where every prior migration is also pending and the "rebuild" is logically a "create with the right shape from the start".
- The migration's own `Apply` function (which often *could* tell, since well-written rebuild migrations check `hasColumn` / `hasTable` and no-op when not needed).

The flag is correctly named for its intent — "this migration rebuilds a whole table, so don't run it without operator approval" — but its enforcement is over-broad. It refuses to run on populations where the rebuild is trivially safe (no rows yet) or trivially unnecessary (table already at target shape).

## Fix: retire `RequiresRebuild` entirely

Replace the gate with a non-negotiable contract for rebuild migrations:

1. **Atomicity**: rebuild migrations MUST wrap their DDL in `BEGIN IMMEDIATE TRANSACTION ... COMMIT`. Concurrent writers block on the RESERVED lock until COMMIT; partial failure ROLLs BACK to the prior shape.
2. **Idempotency**: rebuild migrations MUST check whether the target shape is already in place (`hasColumn` / `hasTable` / `PRAGMA table_info`) and no-op if so. Fresh DBs that come up at the target shape via earlier migrations skip the rebuild trivially. Partial-failure reruns converge.
3. **Backup**: `BackupDB()` continues to run before auto-migrate (`migrate.go:244`), giving a recovery point regardless.

E-1396's V11 already implements all three. The pattern is proven; the framework just needs to stop second-guessing it.

### Changes

- Remove `RequiresRebuild` field from `Migration` struct (migrate.go:33-38).
- Remove the gate at migrate.go:263-270.
- Remove `AllowRebuild` from `MigrateOpts` (migrate.go:75-89), the `RequiresRebuild` references in `MigrateOpts` docs, and the `-force-rebuild` flag from `cmd/endless-event/main.go`.
- Remove `RunnerForceRebuild` from the `RunnerLabel` enum (migrate.go:67-72) and its emission paths.
- Update the `Migration` docstring (migrate.go:18-32) to encode the new contract: rebuild migrations are transactional, idempotent, and operate like any other migration.
- E-1396's V11 is unchanged — it already satisfies the contract.

### Considered alternatives

- **Introspect target shape per-migration via a `NoOpCheck` hook**: surgical, but pushes correctness onto every rebuild-migration author; if they get the check wrong, the safety promise is silently broken.
- **Fresh-DB lineage detection** (auto-allow rebuilds when `user_version = 0` and `_schema_version` is empty): pragmatic but borderline cases (partially migrated DBs from the legacy stopgap) misclassify.
- **Per-call `AllowRebuild` policy plumbed in from the caller**: same lineage-call burden, no new fields.

None of these address the fundamental issue that `RequiresRebuild` doesn't solve a problem the transaction + idempotency contract doesn't already solve. The framework was over-engineered; the right fix is to trust the SQLite primitives.

### What's lost

The "human in the loop" affordance for risky migrations goes away. If a future migration genuinely needs operator scheduling (e.g., a multi-hour rebuild on a large table), it can:

- Use `Target` to gate auto-migrate, so the operator must explicitly bump the version cap, OR
- Live as a separate `endless db migrate-heavy` verb outside the auto-migrate registry, OR
- Be split into smaller idempotent steps that the registry can apply incrementally.

These give the same control without the foot-gun.

## Verification

1. Register a synthetic rebuild migration that's idempotent and transactional. Run the test suite. Expected: all tests pass on fresh DBs.
2. Against an existing DB (user_version=9 from before V10 shipped), invoke auto-migrate via opening any binary. Expected: V10/V11/synthetic apply cleanly.
3. Re-run auto-migrate on a DB already at the target version. Expected: idempotency guard short-circuits; no schema changes.
4. Concurrency: open the DB from two processes simultaneously while V11 (or a synthetic rebuild) is pending. Expected: BEGIN IMMEDIATE serializes; both processes end up at the new schema with no data loss.
5. Add a regression test in `internal/monitor/migrate_test.go` that drives a fresh DB through a registry containing a rebuild migration and asserts the migration applies without operator intervention.

## Related

- E-1396 — surfaced this gap while planning the V11 rebuild; original plan included `RequiresRebuild: true` until it broke 53 tests. V11 already demonstrates the transactional + idempotent pattern this proposal codifies.
- E-1453 — `migrate-db -dry-run` bug, related framework hygiene.
- E-1380 — migration error-handling hygiene, separate scope.

# E-1455 prompt: Use Claude Code env vars as truth for current-pane identification

## Context

**E-1450 (status: ready) addresses the dominant cause** of attribution failures observed during E-1396 verification. E-1450 makes hook-fired writes always target the real DB regardless of sandbox routing, eliminating the cross-DB confusion where a hook wrote a session row to the sandbox DB while the CLI was hitting the real DB. After E-1450 lands, the manual `INSERT INTO sessions ...` workaround used during E-1396 is no longer needed for that case.

What remains is a narrower detection-layer issue, addressed by this task.

## Symptom (post-E-1450)

A Claude Code pane with `CLAUDECODE=1`, `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID=<uuid>`, `TMUX_PANE=%NNN` in its env can still fail attribution in two scenarios that E-1450 doesn't cover:

1. **First-event timing race**: the user opens a Claude pane and immediately runs an `endless` CLI command before Claude has fired its first hook event. The DB has no row yet, so `session_id_resolve()` returns "not a Claude pane" — even though the env vars unambiguously identify this as a Claude pane with a known session_id.
2. **Unnecessary DB round-trips**: every attribution-requiring CLI invocation queries `_live_sessions()` (which shells out to `endless-session-query list-live`) to identify the current pane, even though the env vars in the same process would answer the question directly.

## Root cause

`src/endless/session_cmd.py:1353-1399` (`session_id_resolve()`) and `src/endless/task_cmd.py:2050-2063` (`_find_sibling_claude_session()`) identify Claude panes by DB query, not by env-var inspection. The env vars Claude Code provides are read only in `src/endless/task_cmd.py:231` (`_running_under_agent()` — a yes/no check, not an identity resolver). The DB is treated as the source of truth for "is this a Claude pane and what's its identity," when in fact the env vars in the current process are a fresher, faster, more authoritative answer for the *current* pane (DB queries remain the right answer for *other* panes).

## Fix: env-vars-as-truth for current-pane identification

Make `CLAUDECODE=1` + `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` + `TMUX_PANE` the authoritative signal for "this pane is a Claude session with identity X." The DB becomes a projection of that env-var truth, used only for cross-pane sibling lookup, not for identifying the current pane.

### Changes

1. **`session_cmd.py:session_id_resolve()`**: Check env vars first. If `CLAUDECODE=1` and `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` are set, treat the current pane as a Claude pane with that session_id. Return immediately without consulting the DB for current-pane identification.

2. **Lazy DB-row creation**: If the env-identified session is not in the target DB (first-event-timing case), INSERT a row (`session_id`, `platform='claude'`, `process=TMUX_PANE`, `state='needs_input'`, project_id resolved from cwd) using `INSERT OR IGNORE` keyed on `session_id` to avoid races. This both unblocks the current command and seeds the row for subsequent queries / sibling lookups from other panes.

3. **`task_cmd.py:_find_sibling_claude_session()`**: Cross-pane sibling lookup keeps the DB query (a pane cannot read another pane's env vars). But the current pane's own identity short-circuits via env vars before any sibling search runs. Document the precedence: same-pane → env vars; cross-pane → DB.

4. **`task_cmd.py:_running_under_agent()`**: Stays as-is; already env-var based and used only as a boolean predicate.

### Why this composes with E-1450

After E-1450 lands, hooks always write to the real DB. The lazy INSERT in change #2 above lands in whichever DB the CLI is targeting — which, post-E-1450, is the same DB the hook would have written to anyway. So the env-var path produces the same row the hook would produce, just earlier. Subsequent hook events upsert the same row idempotently. No divergence between the two write paths.

### What this does not do

- Doesn't replace `TouchSession()` in the hook — that stays as the primary registration path for sessions whose lifecycle includes a hook event (which is most of them). The env-var fallback only fires when the resolver runs before any hook event.
- Doesn't reintroduce companion JSON files (retired by E-1426). The env vars are the per-pane source of truth; the DB remains the cross-pane source of truth. This is a layering refinement, not a reversal of E-1426's direction.
- Doesn't address attribution from non-Claude contexts (cron, scripts, plain shells). That's E-1444's `--no-session` flag, complementary.

## Verification

1. From a fresh Claude pane with `CLAUDECODE=1` and `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` set, before the hook has fired any event:
   - Run `endless session id`.
   - Expected: returns the Claude session UUID; the target DB now has a `sessions` row matching.
2. Run `endless decision add ... --about <existing-task>` from the same pane in the same conditions.
   - Expected: succeeds, decision row attributes to the env-identified session.
3. Concurrency: launch two CLIs in the same pane simultaneously before the hook has fired. Expected: no duplicate-key error (INSERT OR IGNORE); single row in `sessions`.
4. Negative: a non-Claude pane (no `CLAUDECODE`) still gets the existing "not a Claude pane" error. Detection must not over-apply.
5. Regression: existing endless-spawn flow (hook has already fired, row exists in DB) keeps working — env-var path just gets there a step earlier without altering the result.
6. Cross-pane sibling: pane A is env-identified as Claude (and lazy-INSERTed its row in step 1). Pane B in the same tmux window runs `endless task bind <id>`. Expected: pane B finds pane A's session via the DB query and binds correctly. (This verifies that the cross-pane path still works when the same-pane shortcut isn't applicable.)
7. With E-1450 not yet landed: the env-var path still works, but the lazy INSERT lands in the sandbox DB (if XDG_CONFIG_HOME routes there) while the user may have wanted the real DB. Document this as "pre-E-1450 behavior; resolves automatically once E-1450 lands."

## Related

- **E-1450** (ready) — makes hook-fired writes always target the real DB. Addresses the cross-DB symptom directly; E-1455 complements it on the detection side. Land E-1450 first; E-1455 is small enough to follow quickly.
- E-1429 — explicit `--db main|worktree|sandbox` routing for self-dev worktrees. Composes cleanly: env-var detection works regardless of which DB the CLI is pointed at.
- E-1419 — "Fix worktree-created companion files being invisible to main-DB session resolution from worktree cwd." Possibly merges with this task — same family of bug; env-vars-as-truth fix likely subsumes it.
- E-1426 — retirement of companion JSON files in favor of DB-as-truth. This proposal does NOT reintroduce companion files; it adds env-vars as a per-pane truth layer on top of DB-as-truth-for-cross-pane.
- E-1444 — `--no-session` flag for attribution bypass. Different angle on the same pain point; useful for cron / scripts where no Claude env vars exist.
- E-1396 — where this was hit during verification.

Implement E-1457 in its own worktree/branch (NOT the e-1449 worktree).

The endless repo has two `.gitignore` problems with Claude Code settings files:

1. **Ignore the local settings files.** `.claude/settings.local.json` (Claude
   Code's per-developer local overrides — local permission allow-rules, etc.)
   is untracked and unignored, showing as `git status` dirt. There are two
   instances today: `.claude/settings.local.json` and
   `demo/.claude/settings.local.json`. Add a single bare line
   `settings.local.json` (NO leading slash) to `.gitignore`. A no-slash pattern
   matches the basename at any depth, so this one line covers both instances and
   any future ones. (The bare basename is intentionally broad; `settings.local.json`
   is exclusively Claude's local-settings convention, so there's no collision
   risk in this repo.)

2. **Remove the dead `.claude/settings.json` entry.** The `.gitignore` currently
   ends the E-998 block with `.claude/settings.json` plus a comment admitting it
   is "mostly documentary — gitignore does not affect tracked files." That entry
   is a no-op: `.claude/settings.json` is a tracked file, so the rule has no
   effect, and it's misleading. Remove the entry. Revise the comment block: drop
   the "documentary" sentence, but keep a short note that `.claude/settings.json`
   is tracked and kept per-worktree via `git update-index --skip-worktree` (run
   by `just claude-settings-init`) — so nobody re-adds a useless gitignore line.
   The skip-worktree mechanism is unaffected by this change.

Verify:
- `git status` in the main checkout no longer lists `.claude/settings.local.json`
  or `demo/.claude/settings.local.json` as untracked.
- `git check-ignore -v .claude/settings.local.json` and
  `git check-ignore -v demo/.claude/settings.local.json` both report the new
  bare rule.
- `.claude/settings.json` is still tracked (`git ls-files .claude/`) and
  worktree regeneration still doesn't dirty status (skip-worktree intact).
- No tracked file named `settings.local.json` exists that the bare pattern would
  unexpectedly start ignoring (`git ls-files | grep settings.local.json` → empty).

This is a small, fully-specified change with no design decisions. Commit on its
own branch, then drive to closure: set the task to `verify` with a
"To verify: ..." handoff and ask Mike whether to land or wait. Do not land
autonomously.

fields.description: The env-var layer of _current_endless_session_id has two defects. (1) It accepts any digit string in ENDLESS_SESSION_ID without checking it matches a sessions-table row, so a bogus or stale value passes the E-1401 gate and misattributes events. (2) The isdigit guard accepts only the integer PK and silently ignores a session UUID, while the gate's remediation message implies any id form works. Survives from obsoleted E-1415 (companion-file portion subsumed by E-1426); defect (2) folded from E-1470. Fix: accept integer PK or UUID (sessions.session_id is UNIQUE), validate against the table, warn and fall through on no match, and align the gate message.
fields.text:
# Validate ENDLESS_SESSION_ID against the sessions table before trusting it

The env-var layer of `_current_endless_session_id` (`src/endless/task_cmd.py`) has two
independent defects, both reachable via `ENDLESS_SESSION_ID`. Both are fixed here.

## Problem 1 — no validation against the sessions table

It accepts any digit string in `ENDLESS_SESSION_ID` and returns it as the current session id without checking that a matching row exists in the `sessions` table:

```python
env_id = os.environ.get("ENDLESS_SESSION_ID")
if env_id and env_id.isdigit():
    return int(env_id)
```

A bogus or stale value (`ENDLESS_SESSION_ID=999999`, or a real id from a since-ended session) passes the E-1401 attribution gate in `event_bridge.py` — the gate only checks that `session_id` is non-empty, not that it's valid — and every event then gets attributed to a non-existent or wrong session. Silent misattribution.

## Problem 2 — only the integer PK is accepted; a UUID is silently ignored (folded from E-1470)

The same `env_id.isdigit()` guard accepts ONLY the integer PK. A Claude session UUID (e.g. `ea2f7409-fd55-48a2-b1a7-9670b9ef9471`) is not all-digits, so it fails the guard and falls through with no signal — even though `sessions.session_id` is UNIQUE and the UUID is the id form a user (or `$CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID`) is most likely to have on hand. Worse, the E-1401 gate's own remediation message ("Export ENDLESS_SESSION_ID=...") implies any id form works, so a user who exports their UUID gets silent non-resolution with no hint why. Surfaced during E-1470's resolver investigation and folded here per Mike (2026-05-25).

## Origin

This was originally part of E-1415 ("Validate resolved session id against DB in `_current_endless_session_id`"). E-1415 was set to `obsolete`: its companion-file-validation portion is subsumed by E-1426 (which retires companion files — once the resolver reads `sessions.process` directly, a row read from the DB inherently exists). The **env-var layer is independent of companion files** and that concern survives E-1415's obsoletion — hence this dedicated task. Problem 2 is a separate defect in the same guard, split out of E-1470.

## Fix

In `_current_endless_session_id`, replace the bare `isdigit()` acceptance with a lookup that accepts EITHER id form and confirms the row exists:
- Integer PK: look up the id in `sessions`.
- UUID: look up `sessions.session_id` (UNIQUE) and resolve to its integer PK.

If the value matches no row (either form):
- Treat the env-var layer as not-resolved (fall through to the next resolution layer).
- Write a one-line warning to stderr naming the bad value: e.g. `warning: ENDLESS_SESSION_ID=999999 does not match any session; ignoring.`

Do not silently accept it, and do not silently discard it without telling the user — a stale env var in their shell is something they need to know about and fix.

Also align the E-1401 gate's remediation message with what's actually accepted (integer PK or UUID), so the suggested `Export ENDLESS_SESSION_ID=...` is not misleading.

## Coordination

- **E-1426** (urgent, in progress) rewrites `_current_endless_session_id`'s companion/process layer. This task touches the same function's *env-var* layer (layer 1, which runs before the process lookup). Whoever holds E-1426 should be aware this validation is landing in the same function; sequence to avoid a merge collision.
- **E-1401** is the attribution gate this validation protects. The gate itself stays a presence check; this task makes the resolver stop feeding it invalid ids, and fixes the gate's misleading remediation-message wording (Problem 2).

## Verification

1. `ENDLESS_SESSION_ID=999999 endless task add "x"` from a context with no other resolution layer available → resolver ignores the bogus value, stderr warns, and the E-1401 gate refuses (no misattribution).
2. `ENDLESS_SESSION_ID=<valid live integer id>` → resolver returns it, no warning.
3. `ENDLESS_SESSION_ID=<valid live session UUID>` → resolver resolves it to the integer PK, no warning (Problem 2).
4. `ENDLESS_SESSION_ID=<id of an ended session>` → treated as invalid (decide during planning whether "ended" counts as invalid, or only "no row at all"; present the choice to Mike).
5. Tests covering valid-integer / valid-UUID / non-existent / malformed env-var values.

fields.description: Eliminate per-task tasks.prompt content. Render the spawn handoff from a single template merged with task metadata (id, title) and runtime context (spawning pane id). The handoff is mostly boilerplate (orient, default interaction rules, closing) — generating it removes the prompt-vs-plan drift entirely since agents no longer write prompts. Drops the tasks.prompt column, the --prompt flag on task add/update, and reshapes 'endless task prompt' into 'endless task handoff' which renders the template instead of displaying a stored field.

fields.title: Stop task update --text from creating a worktree; materialize plan only at claim

fields.outcome: Superseded by E-1445, which implements the decided change: task update --text never creates a worktree (plan materializes only at claim). The 'should the default flip?' question E-1276 deferred is now answered.

payload.title: Sync just go and test-go recipes with the full Go binary and package set
payload.description: `just go` builds only 5 of the 7 Go binaries — it omits endless-session-query and endless-tmux, which only `just build` builds. So quick dev iteration via `just go` leaves those binaries stale/missing on PATH (this bit E-1445: the new `endless-session-query task-text` subcommand wasn't built by `just go`). Separately, `just test-go` runs only internal/kairos, internal/events, and cmd/endless-sandbox — it does not cover internal/monitor (e.g. the new monitor.TaskText reader) or cmd/endless-session-query, so query-binary logic has no automated Go test. Align both recipes with the real binary/package set so quick dev builds and the Go test sweep cover everything `just build` produces. Surfaced during E-1445.

payload.title: Prevent worktree land from advancing main before the worktree-clean check
payload.description: endless worktree land fast-forwarded main to the task branch tip, then errored on an uncommitted worktree file (an untracked materialized plan), leaving main advanced while the worktree remained and the branch's later commits unmerged (observed landing E-1429, 2026-05-25). Land must verify all preconditions (clean worktree, etc.) BEFORE advancing main, or roll main back on failure, so a failed land leaves no half-applied state.
